top_profane.text:
So I've been hearing about other reviews for this game and how it's been averaging fairly low (5-6 out of 10) with "professional" game reviewers. Remember, folks, the word "professional" just means that someone is paid to do something...not that they are necessarily good at it.

First and foremost, realize a title like WKC isn't hyped enough for the publisher to bribe video game review websites for a decent review (unlike Grand Theft Auto IV or Modern Warfare 2). Also...if you think IGN or Gamespot or any other review website isn't "bribed" in some way to ignore or make light of a highly hyped game's sucky spots then I want some of the kool-aid you're drinking so that I can ride home on the mothership with you.

With that being said, let's get down to the game.

The Good:

Graphics - The latest consoles have pretty much spoiled us. We expect greatness and beauty and this game delivers. The characters are very detailed and the environments are rich and colorful. I am still amazed at how well the latest games can capture the look of water. The devil is in the details and WKC delivers very well.

Sound - The voice acting is about par for a decent JRPG. There are some seriously corny parts but if you enjoy Japanese RPGs and anime then everything will mesh perfectly. The music is very lovely and flows right along with the ga
top_profane.title: If you like JRPGs then WKC is your game!

I did a lot of research before buying this mouse about 9 months ago. I really liked the amount of features and customization you could do with it. But I also read a lot of reviews talking about how the WoW app is a glitchy piece of crap (it is), and that the mouse stops tracking after a few months (it did).

I haven't even had this 100+ dollar thing for more that a year and already the laser stops tracking either horizontally or vertically at random. It started about 3 months after I bought it (just beyond warranty range), it was pretty rare, maybe once or twice a week. Now it's every day, multiple times a day. The only way to get it to stop screwing up, is to beat the crap out of it. I generally don't like to have to beat the crap out of something I paid $120 for, just to keep it functioning.

When it WORKS, the design is odd but not un-intuitive. And the side-hat button, and various other buttons worked really great for an mmo class with a lot of moves to track (a hunter in WoW). Granted, as I said, the WoW mod that comes with it is useless because it constantly forgets how you set it up. However, once you get used to programming hotkeys, and macros yourself, it becomes very easy to be dependent on the many things this mouse can do. Which just makes it all the more sad that it's build quality is such crap.

Before I even try to review, I'd like to take some time, to thank about 300 complete morons, for spamming this review area, with some of the worst thought out posts, this side of the Gamefaqs boards.

Now then, lets get to brass tacks-

DS vs PSP. Why would you even consider comparing them. DS was designed to be the next step up from the GBA. PSP was designed to be a all in one, portable media experience. The hardware, and software is so different, that even attempting to compare, or call one better then the other, based on "graphics" or "bugs" is redundent and a true sign of an immature reviewer.

Since everyone seems to think the PSP is God when it comes to hand-held gaming, allow me to break the mythos right now. It isn't. The PSP, while being a sleek and stylish peice of hardware, goes for looks over substance, and thus, thanks to some really shoddy placement of controls, and no consideration for long term gaming(try playing a game for more then 3 hours, and tell me your hands don't hurt), it loses points in the overall score, and scheam of things.

Graphic wise however, it's in a league of its own. Sorry Nintendo fans and faithful followers, but the DS can not compete with PSP visuals. It can however, stay in the game by offering thrills, that we have all come to expect, from a hand-held gaming experience.

Time for another

This game is really great. Very complex, quite in depth, very challenging, and so cool, that I've not yet had the chance to enjoy it in its true form...

Origin is like a Chinese version of Steam. It's simple, it's slow, it's made cheaply, and it sorta works. The support is terrible, and pretty much no help at all. Companies complain that Steam charges too much, and EA/Maxis doesn't want to pay those high prices. Well, this is one of those "You get what you pay for" situations. Origin is crap, and everything with the launch of this game was a failure. And I'm not even talking about the game itself yet.

The problem with this game is the network synchronization implementation. First, they have been referring to is as being in the cloud. I'm not sure who at Maxis got the definition of cloud computing confused with an oldskool WoW way of doing things, but this sure as hell is no cloud implementation. On release day, there was one east USA server, and one West USA server, as well as several other worldwide servers. Each server is separate from the other. They do not sync data between themselves. Therefore, your game on one server, cannot be retrieved if that server is too busy. Low and behold, they added two more US servers, and more Europe servers. Still didn't fix the issue. They pushed out a patch, said to significantly increase performance, but yet here I si
top_profane.title: Such an awesome vision, but the implementation is a failure

I paid over $100 for this controller, so to say I want to love it would be an understatement. Sadly, however, this is the worst Microsoft product I've ever owned, and I used Windows Me *and* Vista - who knew Microsoft could go lower than those steaming piles of awful, awful quality? Well, they did here. Here's a place where Microsoft engineers could have raised an aging company from the ashes like a majestic Phoenix and proved their sheer genius with a masterpiece of gaming technology. All the Xbox One Elite wireless controller is, however, is crap. Complete and utter crap.

The feels, man...the feels is great. Both in my gaming swagger and in my hands - I feel like a million bucks when I hold this thing...wait, I feel like I'm holding a million bucks because the second mortgage that I had to take out on my house to pay for this controller is due. If anyone is interested, I'm looking for angel investors to fund the cost of a replacement, by the way. So let's get down to the review. No.

Grand Theft Auto IV receives a lot of flack that I think is unwarranted. People complain that this game isn't fun, that it isn't super crazy and whacky like past games. If you want an over-the-top adventure, go play Saint's Row. Grand Theft Auto IV is a very fun game and it may not deserve the 10/10's that it got but I still think it deserves to be one of the highest rated games of the generation.

Grand Theft Auto IV brings a sense of realism to the franchise that was previously less prevalent. You will play as Niko Bellic, an Eastern European immigrant who finds his way to Liberty City (New York City) for a specific reason. He goes to see his cousin, Roman, but there may also be an ulterior motive there besides visiting a relative who claims to have found fame and fortune in America, although you will immediately see that he is eeking out a living in squalor. Niko sets about changing that and earning the Cousins Bellic the money they need to live comfortably in America. The story is great. The poetic tale about Niko, Roman and the American Dream is beautiful. You see, every GTA game has been about achieving the American Dream through criminal means, GTA IV is no different but it shows a different perspective. Niko doesn't like America, he despises our culture and annoying citizens, he longs for the old country and the simpler days but laments that he can never have

why does everyone keep saying this game is EXTREMELY HARD? and for that guy that said it took him 2 hours to get past the second level boss...that is pathetic, especially if you practiced for a week on the original.

anyway, i haven't beat the game yet, but i'm on the last mission, which is just the last boss fight, so i think i can write a review now.

first off, the game kicks ass. everything from the style, to the cutscenes, to the in-game action is top-notch. i found myself beating the levels, not just to progress in the game, but also to see what the next cut-scene revealed. this game actually has a story, and it has its corny moments, but you just have to understand that this is just how things are going to be and deal with it. the over-the-top action in the cut-scenes blew me away a few times, even the opening cutscene to the first level rocked my socks off and got me in the mood to kick some demonic ass. the character models in this game are fairly well done, the main characters anyway, and the bosses. the normal enemies are kind of low in the polygon count, but that's okay, because the point of the game isn't to stand around and look at them, it's to shoot and slice them up in the coolest possible manner. the voice-acting in the cutscenes is surprisingly good, and the motion-captured movements are some of the best yet in any game.

When NIS first announced Prinny: Can I Really Be The Hero?, I was worried that it was going to be a half-assed attempt to milk the Disgaea cow. However, after playing the game, I'm impressed how much effort and attention to detail they put into this game. The back of the box claims that this is "their first ever action game", but it feels like they have years of experience and expertise in making side-scrolling platformers.

MUSIC

Prinny re-uses a lot of music from the original Disgaea, which is a good thing since the original Disgaea had some good music in it. The original songs are also pretty good, too, though in my completely objective opinion, it's not Tenpei Sato's best work. That said, I do not feel the need to make the distinction between fantastic and awesome.

Of course, being an action game, you're generally so busy trying not to get killed that you don't really have the chance to stop and enjoy the music. That said, Prinny is one of the few action games fortunate enough to have a J-RPG composer writing music for them, and it really shows. (Or sounds. Whatever.)

STORY

Like the rest of the Disgaea series, Prinny is a farce. The plot is irrelevant. It's all about the silly, crazy, stupidly ridiculous things that the game throws at you. In a good way. And being an action game, it doesn't really matter, because the sto
top_five_star_obscene.title: A Review of Prinny: Can I Really Be The Hero?

I run a large urban garbage removal service in a major metropolitan area, and let me be the first to tell you that this product is hands-down the best garbage truck simulator available on the market. You may be thinking that 36 big ones is exorbitant for a program that is probably shovelware from an unknown developer and publisher, but I use it to train each and every one of my employees.

Vigorously.

I'm talking a full room with black walls that double as screens. Each trainee gets a full 360 display of a virtual city as they grab on to a black beam with a handle and a controller (which of course serves as the back end of a truck). We pump in a steady stream of chilled methane, and keep the city display dim. This all mimics the experience of collecting trash in the wee hours of the morning. Each trainee wears a full motion-capturing cat-suit that feeds movement into the Garbage Truck Simulator, and that baby pumps out raw data like a sewage pipe. It analyzes and corrects trash can throwing motions, wind resistance and the drag generated by body positioning on the back of the truck, heck, even the arc of their stream when they make water in small alleyways and window boxes. This program does it all.

And heck, that's only half of it. Garbage Truck Simulator is also a driving sim, for the guys who actually drive the trucks. My colleagues and I figu

The first and most obvious thing to compliment this game on is its look. It's based on 1930s cartoons and is HAND DRAWN FRAME BY FRAME. Imagine that! It's mindblowing the amount of work that went into this game. And it nails the presentation. The music, look, characters, it's all oozing with flavor and style.

The gameplay is excellent as well. It's a platformer/shooter. It plays much like games such as megaman and contra. It's mainly boss focused though, so lots of that. Some standard levels thrown in but not nearly as many as the boss fights.

Now lets talk about the difficulty. There's 2 settings, regular and simple. Regular is brutally difficult for me, and I'm good at platformers generally. Be prepared to die atleast 10 times on your way to defeat a boss. It takes a lot of deaths to learn the patterns of each one, there's a lot of trial and error involved. But even though this is brutally difficult, it's not unfair or cheap. That makes it very rewarding once you actually defeat a boss. But be aware, this game is probably too difficult for small children, or casual gamers. The developers had to make the game difficult, or else you'd be able to blow through it too quickly - this hand drawn style is far too time consuming to produce and as such can't offer a huge volume of content as other games can. If you hypothetically were to never die, you could proba
